attributes.stem: A 23-year-old woman presents with colicky abdominal pain and distension in the setting of known ileocolonic Crohn's disease. The pain is located in the right lower quadrant and associated with non-bloody diarrhoea. She has experienced similar episodes before, each lasting several days. The physical examination is unremarkable, apart from the presence of some perianal skin tags.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation in evaluating the severity of disease?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is likely suffering from a relapse of Crohn's disease. This is most likely affecting the terminal ileum, evidenced by the pain localised to the right iliac fossa.

Endoscopy and ileocolonoscopy with small bowel biopsy are used in the initial diagnosis of Crohn's disease. However, this patient is already known to have Crohn's disease and now the aim is to classify the site and extent of disease activity.

CT abdomen and magnetic resonance enterography (MRE) and enteroclysis are the imaging modalities most commonly used in evaluation of Crohn's disease. CT and MRE are equally accurate, however MRE is preferred given the reduced radiation and hence reduced risk of radiation-associated strictures. This is especially important as Crohn's disease often proceeds on a chronic course, with lifelong imaging required. MRE can distinguish between fibrosis and active inflammation and assess the full thickness of the bowel, which will help evaluate response to treatment. MRE is very helpful in restaging disease and planning any change in therapy.

Plain abdominal X-ray +/- barium swallow may be used to detect fistulas or stenosis in a patient presenting with complications of Crohn's disease, however MRE would be preferred for more precise information about restaging disease in this case.

Whilst capsule endoscopy provides useful assessment of many small bowel pathologies, it will only assess the mucosa. Additionally, there is a risk of the capsule becoming impacted if there is fibrotic narrowing of the lumen of the small bowel.
learningPoints: A magnetic resonance enterography (MRE) performed in Crohn's disease can distinguish between fibrosis and active inflammation and assess the full thickness of the bowel, which will help evaluate response to treatment. MRE is very helpful in restaging disease and planning any change in therapy.
references: Al-Hindawi A, Appadurai V, Ingham G, Seleem M. A challenging case of chronic diarrhoea. Aust Fam Physician. 2015 Jan-Feb;44(1-2):59-62. PMID: 25688964.

Bettenworth D, Bokemeyer A, Baker M, Mao R, Parker CE, Nguyen T, Ma C, Panés J, Rimola J, Fletcher JG, Jairath V, Feagan BG, Rieder F; Stenosis Therapy and Anti-Fibrotic Research (STAR) Consortium.. Assessment of Crohn's disease-associated small bowel strictures and fibrosis on cross-sectional imaging: a systematic review. Gut. 2019 Jun;68(6):1115-1126. doi: 10.1136/gutjnl-2018-318081. Epub 2019 Apr 3. PMID: 30944110; PMCID: PMC6580870.

Amitai MM, Ben-Horin S, Eliakim R, Kopylov U. Magnetic resonance enterography in Crohn's disease: a guide to common imaging manifestations for the IBD physician. J Crohns Colitis. 2013 Sep;7(8):603-15. doi: 10.1016/j.crohns.2012.10.005. Epub 2012 Nov 1. PMID: 23122965.

Moy MP, Sauk J, Gee MS. The Role of MR Enterography in Assessing Crohn's Disease Activity and Treatment Response. Gastroenterol Res Pract. 2016;2016:8168695. doi: 10.1155/2016/8168695. Epub 2015 Dec 27. PMID: 26819611; PMCID: PMC4706951.
